questions.question: Explain the difference between a system call and a library function, giving one example of each.
keyPoints: System calls transition to kernel mode; library functions run in user mode
System calls request OS services directly; library functions provide convenient abstractions
Examples: read(), write(), fork() are system calls; printf(), fopen(), strcpy() are library functions
Library functions often internally use system calls

questions.question: What is errno and how is it used for error handling in Unix system programming?
keyPoints: errno is a global variable that stores error codes from failed system calls
Defined in <errno.h> header
When a system call fails (returns -1), errno is set to indicate specific error
Macros like EACCES, ENOENT, EBUSY represent different error conditions
Functions like perror() and strerror() convert errno to readable messages

questions.question: Describe the three standard file descriptors in Unix and their purposes.
keyPoints: 0 = STDIN (standard input) - for reading input
1 = STDOUT (standard output) - for normal output
2 = STDERR (standard error) - for error messages
These are automatically opened for every process
File descriptors are small non-negative integers used to reference open files

questions.question: What is the role of the Unix kernel? Mention at least four major responsibilities.
keyPoints: Process scheduling - decides which process runs when
Memory management - allocates/deallocates RAM for processes
File system management - handles reading/writing files
Device management - interfaces with hardware through drivers
Provides inter-process communication mechanisms
Acts as interface between user programs and hardware

questions.question: Explain the concept of user mode versus kernel mode in Unix.
keyPoints: User mode: unprivileged mode where user programs run
Kernel mode: privileged mode with full hardware access
User programs cannot directly access hardware; must use system calls
Kernel mode protected by hardware to ensure system stability
Transition between modes involves context switching overhead

questions.question: What is POSIX and why is it important for Unix system programming?
keyPoints: POSIX = Portable Operating System Interface
Standard defined by IEEE for Unix-like systems
Ensures portability across different Unix implementations (Linux, BSD, macOS)
Defines API standards including system calls, shell utilities
Programs written to POSIX standards can compile on any POSIX-compliant system

questions.question: List and briefly explain the three main categories of Unix system calls.
keyPoints: Process Control: fork, exec, wait, exit - manage process creation/termination
File Management: open, read, write, close, mkdir, rmdir - handle file operations
Communication: pipe, socket, msgget, shmget - enable inter-process communication
Additional categories: Device, Information Maintenance, Protection

questions.question: Describe the execution flow when a user program makes a system call to read from a file. Include the transition between user mode and kernel mode in your explanation.
keyPoints: 1. Program calls read() library function with file descriptor, buffer, count
2. Library function (in user space) prepares parameters and invokes syscall instruction
3. CPU switches from user mode to kernel mode (privileged)
4. Kernel validates parameters, locates file descriptor in process table
5. Kernel invokes device driver to read data from hardware
6. Data copied from kernel buffer to user buffer
7. Return to user mode with byte count read (or -1 on error)
8. If error, errno is set; program checks return value

questions.question: Write a C program that opens a file, reads its contents, and handles potential errors using errno. Explain your error handling approach.
keyPoints: Include <stdio.h>, <fcntl.h>, <unistd.h>, <errno.h>, <stdlib.h>
Use open() with O_RDONLY flag
Check if open() returns -1 (failure); use perror() to print error message
Use read() in a loop to read file contents
Check read() return value: -1 = error, 0 = EOF, >0 = bytes read
Close file descriptor with close()
Always check return values from system calls
Use errno to determine specific failure reason

questions.question: Explain the Unix philosophy and how it influences system program design. Provide examples of how this philosophy is applied in Unix utilities.
keyPoints: Do one thing well - each program has single, focused purpose
Small is beautiful - prefer small, simple programs over large ones
Use flat text files - human-readable format enables easy processing
Chain programs with pipes - combine simple tools for complex operations
Examples: grep (search), sed (stream editor), awk (text processing)
Design enables building complex pipelines: cat file | grep pattern | sort | uniq
